nodes.label: Bounded thread-safe message queue
nodes.rationale: Needs a mutex plus two condition variables (not_empty, not_full), not just a mutex, so producer/consumer threads block correctly when the queue is empty/full; it is the one core primitive everything else in radio_sim builds on.

nodes.label: Pub/sub layer (topic registry, fan-out)
nodes.rationale: Publishing clones the message into every subscriber's own queue rather than calling handlers directly, so one slow subscriber can never stall another.

nodes.label: HELLO/HELLO_ACK/HELLO_NACK protocol version handshake
nodes.rationale: Plain text, line-based, deliberately not JSON, to avoid a chicken-and-egg problem of needing to already agree on a format before agreeing on one. Connection-level negotiation fits a persistent Unix socket better than per-message versioning (used on connectionless transports like UDP), since there is a well-defined moment (connection setup) to agree on a version once.

nodes.label: Schema version 2 (JSON telemetry, additive + breaking change)
nodes.rationale: Deliberately includes both kinds of schema change: the new temperature_c field is additive/backward-compatible (a v1-only client never sees it), while ts moving from a Unix epoch integer to an ISO-8601 string is a breaking change a v1 client would fail to parse -- exactly why the handshake matters.
nodes.norm_label: schema version 2 (json telemetry, additive + breaking change)

nodes.label: Protobuf wire-format upgrade
nodes.rationale: Build the JSON version first and swap the wire format to protobuf as its own deliberate later exercise, so migrating a working wire format can be practiced and compared directly against the JSON approach. Protobuf handles additive schema change for free via field numbers, but a real breaking change (like the ts type change) still isn't safe in place -- you'd retire the field number or lean on the connection-level handshake.

nodes.label: 4-byte length-prefix framing
nodes.rationale: Protobuf gives compact bytes but no built-in message boundaries on a stream socket; a 4-byte big-endian length prefix before each serialized Envelope is the standard fix, and both the C writer and Python reader must agree on it explicitly.

nodes.label: Pre-allocated fixed-size message pool
nodes.rationale: Replaces malloc/free in the message hot path with acquire/release against a fixed-size, pre-allocated pool, avoiding unbounded allocator time and long-run heap fragmentation the way real embedded firmware does. Trade-off: pre-committed worst-case memory and a closed/size-capped set of message shapes (the pool must be sized to the largest of telemetry/alert payloads); not the default choice for a general-purpose backend service.

nodes.label: Pool exhaustion policy
nodes.rationale: msg_pool_acquire returns NULL on exhaustion; the caller drops and logs/counts the message rather than blocking or falling back to malloc, matching msg_queue_try_enqueue's non-blocking drop-and-move-on precedent. A malloc fallback would quietly defeat the entire point of the pool.

nodes.label: SIGINT clean shutdown
nodes.rationale: A SIGINT handler signals all threads to stop, drains queues, and joins every thread before exiting -- worth building deliberately since 'how do you shut down a multithreaded system cleanly' is a very plausible question in its own right.

nodes.label: Phase 1 -- foundational queue + pub/sub
nodes.rationale: Stop here and it's already worth something: pthread_create, mutex/cond-var discipline, and pub/sub fan-out are all demonstrable from code that actually runs. Realistic minimum bar for the whole project.

nodes.label: Clean SIGINT shutdown
nodes.rationale: main blocks SIGINT in every thread before spawning any of them and waits on it synchronously via sigwait(), then orchestrates teardown explicitly: msg_queue_shutdown broadcasts to wake every thread blocked in a queue enqueue/dequeue, shutdown_requested() is a polled flag covering waits a queue can't reach (producer's between-tick sleep, transport/command listener poll() loops), and everything is joined before the process exits.

nodes.label: WSL build requirement
nodes.rationale: Needs real POSIX headers (sys/socket.h, sys/un.h for AF_UNIX, a SIGPIPE-having signal.h) that native Windows/MinGW-w64 gcc doesn't provide no matter what flags are passed, so the project must be built and run inside WSL2 Ubuntu instead.
